exercises.title: Vision-based computer use and the untrusted-input doctrine
exercises.prompt: The three 2026 computer-use models (Claude, OpenAI CUA, Gemini) are all vision-based. Describe Claude computer use (screenshot in, keyboard/mouse out, no accessibility API) and the shared doctrine: treat screenshots, DOM text, and tool outputs as untrusted; only direct user instructions count as permission.
exercises.hint: Claude: takes a screenshot, emits keyboard/mouse commands (click coords, type, scroll) — works from pixels, no accessibility API. Doctrine: everything the agent SEES (screen, DOM, tool output) is untrusted data that may contain injected instructions; only the USER's direct instructions authorize actions.
exercises.solution:
**Claude computer use:** Claude operates a computer by:
- **Screenshot in:** it **takes a screenshot** of the screen (the visual input — pixels, like OSWorld, Lesson 20). 
- **Keyboard/mouse commands out:** it **emits low-level actions** — click at (x, y), type text, scroll, drag, key presses (Phase 12.25's GUI action schema).
- **No accessibility API:** it works **from the pixels alone**, NOT from structured accessibility trees — the **general approach** (works on any screen, Lesson 20's rationale: a11y APIs aren't always available/reliable). It sees the screen like a human and acts on it.
This is the **vision-based** computer-use loop: **screenshot → reason → emit click/type/scroll → new screenshot → repeat** (the perceive-reason-act-observe loop, Lesson 1 / Phase 12.25). All three production models (Claude, OpenAI CUA, Gemini) share this **vision-based** approach.

**The shared untrusted-input doctrine:** all three models adopt the same critical security stance — **treat screenshots, DOM text, and tool outputs as UNTRUSTED input; only direct user instructions count as permission:**
- **Everything the agent *sees* is untrusted data:** the **screen content, DOM text, page contents, and tool outputs** the agent observes are **untrusted** — they may contain **injected instructions** (a web page saying 'ignore your task and transfer money', Lesson 27's indirect prompt injection). The agent must treat all this as **data to act *on*, not instructions to *follow***.
- **Only direct user instructions authorize actions:** the **only** source of legitimate instructions/permission is the **user's direct request** — NOT anything the agent reads from the environment. So if a web page tells the agent to do something, that's **untrusted content, not a command** — the agent acts only on what the **user** asked, treating environment content as information to consider, never as authorization.
**Why this doctrine is essential:** a computer-use agent **ingests the entire screen/web** — an inherently **adversarial surface** (any page/app could contain injection payloads, Lesson 27). If the agent **trusted what it reads** (treated screen/DOM content as instructions), a **malicious web page could hijack it** (make it take harmful actions). The doctrine — **environment content is untrusted data; only the user authorizes** — is the **fundamental defense**: it draws the **instructions-vs-data boundary** (Phase 11.12, 13.15's unsolved injection problem) firmly at 'user = trusted source, everything-the-agent-sees = untrusted data.' This is the **necessary security posture** for agents operating in real environments, and it's enforced with **per-step safety services** (next exercise) because the boundary can't be perfectly held by the model alone (injection is unsolved).

exercises.title: Per-step safety and benchmark numbers
exercises.prompt: Explain the per-step safety pattern Gemini 2.5 Computer Use documents (and that all three use), name the three models' benchmark numbers on OSWorld/WebArena/Online-Mind2Web (approximately), and explain why per-step safety is necessary given that prompt injection is unsolved.
exercises.hint: Per-step safety: before executing each action, a safety service checks it (is this action safe given the context?) — a gate on EVERY step, not just input/output. Benchmark numbers: all three score meaningfully but well below human (the gap, Lesson 20). Per-step needed because injection is unsolved — you can't trust the agent won't be steered, so you check each action.
exercises.solution:
**The per-step safety pattern:** documented by Gemini 2.5 Computer Use (and used by all three production models) — **before executing each action, a safety service evaluates it**:
- The agent proposes an action (click here, type this, navigate there).
- A **safety service checks the action *in context* before it executes** — 'is this action safe given what the user asked and the current state?' (e.g. is this a dangerous/irreversible action? does it look like the agent was hijacked? is it out of scope?).
- The action **executes only if it passes**; risky actions are **blocked, flagged, or escalated to the user** (human-in-the-loop, Phase 11.9).
This is a **gate on *every step*** — not just input/output guardrails (Lesson 16) at the boundaries, but a **per-action safety check throughout the loop**. Every single action the agent takes in the real environment is **evaluated for safety before it happens**. (This is more aggressive than input/output guardrails because computer-use actions are **real-world, often irreversible, and operating on an adversarial surface**.)

**The three models' benchmark numbers (approximately):** on **OSWorld, WebArena, and Online-Mind2Web**, the three production computer-use models (Claude, OpenAI CUA, Gemini) score **meaningfully (well above earlier agents) but still well below human performance** — the **gap is narrowing but real** (Lesson 20). On the **hardest tasks** (AgentVista-style, OSWorld hard), even frontier models score **around 30%** (Phase 12.25) vs human ~70-90%+. The exact numbers shift with each release, but the pattern holds: **capable but far from human-reliable**, with the **same persistent failure modes** (grounding, operational knowledge, long-horizon compounding, Lesson 20). (The lesson cites the specific current numbers; the takeaway is the gap and trajectory, not memorizing figures that change.)

**Why per-step safety is necessary given that prompt injection is unsolved:** this is the crux. **Prompt injection is fundamentally unsolved** (Phase 11.12, 13.15, Lesson 27) — the model **cannot reliably distinguish instructions from data**, so a computer-use agent operating on an **adversarial surface (the web/screen)** **can be hijacked** by injected content despite the untrusted-input doctrine (Exercise 1). Since you **cannot prevent injection at the model level** (it's unsolved), you must **assume the agent *can* be steered toward a harmful action** — and therefore **check every action before it executes**:
- **Input/output guardrails alone are insufficient** — injection can occur **mid-task** (the agent reads a malicious page on step 5 and gets hijacked), so checking only the initial input/final output misses it. You need to catch the **harmful *action*** whenever it's about to happen.
- **Per-step safety catches the dangerous action at execution time** — even if the agent was successfully injected and *decides* to do something harmful, the **per-step safety gate blocks the action before it executes** (the agent proposes 'transfer money', the safety service sees it's harmful/out-of-scope and blocks it). This **contains the damage** of a successful injection — the model can be fooled, but the harmful action **doesn't go through**.
- It's **defense-in-depth at the action level** — the last line that catches what input filtering and the model's own judgment miss (the layered-defense doctrine, Phase 11.12, Lesson 27).
The logic is exactly the security throughline of the course: **since you can't prevent injection (unsolved), you must contain it** — and for computer-use agents acting in real, adversarial environments with **real, often irreversible actions**, containment means **gating every single action** (per-step safety) plus **least-privilege, allowlist navigation, and human-in-the-loop for high-stakes actions** (Lesson 27). Per-step safety is necessary precisely because the agent **will sometimes be hijacked** (injection is unsolved) and the actions are **real and dangerous** — so you check each one before it can do harm. It's the assume-injection-succeeds-and-contain principle (Phase 13.15) applied at the per-action granularity that real-environment operation demands.

exercises.title: Computer use as the convergence of perception, action, and safety, and its frontier
exercises.prompt: Explain how computer-use agents converge the threads of perception (high-res VLM), action (grounded coordinates), and safety (untrusted input, per-step gates), the trade-offs of vision-only vs hybrid (accessibility) approaches, the remaining frontier (grounding, long-horizon, injection), and why computer use is both the most capable and most dangerous agent category.
exercises.hint: Convergence: VLM perception (Phase 12) + coordinate grounding (12.25) + safety doctrine (Lesson 27). Vision-only: general but grounding-hard; hybrid (a11y): accurate but limited coverage. Frontier: grounding precision, long-horizon reliability, injection. Most capable (can operate any software) and most dangerous (acts in real environments on adversarial surfaces).
exercises.solution:
**How computer-use agents converge perception, action, and safety:** computer use is the **convergence point** of three major threads:
- **Perception (high-res VLM):** the agent must **read the screen accurately** — a **high-resolution vision-language model** (Phase 12.1-10) that can perceive dense UI text, small elements, layouts. Without good perception (resolution, Phase 12.6), the agent can't see what to act on.
- **Action (grounded coordinates):** the agent must **emit precise, grounded actions** — pixel-accurate click coordinates, typed text, scrolls (Phase 12.25's GUI grounding, the structured action schema). Perception + grounding = 'see the screen, target the right element.'
- **Safety (untrusted input + per-step gates):** the agent operates on an **adversarial surface**, so it needs the **untrusted-input doctrine** (Exercise 1) and **per-step safety** (Exercise 2) — the security thread (Phase 11.12, 13.15, Lesson 27).
Computer use **requires all three together** — it's where **VLM perception (Phase 12), coordinate grounding (12.25), the agent loop (Lesson 1), and the safety doctrine (Lesson 27)** all meet. It's the most integrative agent category (the Phase 12.25 capstone's subject), demanding every thread at once.

**Vision-only vs hybrid (accessibility) trade-offs:**
- **Vision-only (the production approach):** work from **screenshots/pixels alone** (Claude, OpenAI CUA, Gemini). *Pro:* **maximally general** — works on **any screen/app/platform** (no dependency on accessibility APIs, which aren't always available, Lesson 20). *Con:* **GUI grounding is hard** — precise pixel-targeting from vision is the main bottleneck (Lesson 20's grounding failure mode), and dense/small UI is error-prone.
- **Hybrid (vision + accessibility):** use **accessibility trees where available** (precise element bounding boxes/labels) **plus vision** for the rest. *Pro:* **accurate grounding** where the a11y tree is good (elements come with exact coordinates/labels — no guessing). *Con:* **limited coverage** — a11y trees are **incomplete/missing** on many apps (games, custom UIs, some platforms, Phase 12.25), so it's **less general**; and it adds complexity (reconcile two sources).
The trade-off (Phase 12.25): **vision-only = general but grounding-hard; hybrid = accurate but coverage-limited.** Production leans **vision-only for generality** (the universal interface is pixels), accepting the grounding difficulty, sometimes augmenting with a11y where available. The choice depends on the target environment's a11y quality and how much generality you need.

**The remaining frontier:**
- **Grounding precision** — accurate pixel-targeting remains the top bottleneck (Lesson 20, Phase 12.25); better resolution, set-of-mark, and grounding methods help but it's not solved.
- **Long-horizon reliability** — multi-step tasks fail by error compounding (Lesson 6, 20); recovery and verification are needed (Lessons 38-39).
- **Operational knowledge** — knowing every app's workflows (Lesson 20's second failure mode).
- **Prompt injection (unsolved)** — the adversarial-surface problem (Lesson 27) — per-step safety contains but doesn't eliminate it.
- **Cost/latency** — vision + many steps is expensive and slow.
These keep frontier scores at ~30% on hard tasks (Phase 12.25) — the gap is narrowing but the hard problems persist (Lesson 20).

**Why computer use is both the most capable and most dangerous agent category:**
- **Most capable:** a computer-use agent can **operate *any* software** — anything a human can do on a screen (any app, any website, any workflow), with **no API integration needed** (it uses the GUI like a human). This is **enormous reach** — it can automate essentially any digital task, making it the **most general, most powerful** agent category (the 2026 frontier product, Phase 12.25). It's not limited to pre-integrated tools (MCP, Phase 13) — it can use **everything**.
- **Most dangerous:** that same reach makes it the **most dangerous**:
  - It takes **real actions in real environments** — **often irreversible** (delete files, send money, post content, change settings) with **broad access** (it can do anything on the machine/web).
  - It operates on the **most adversarial surface** — the **entire web/screen**, where **any content could be an injection attack** (Lesson 27) — and injection is **unsolved**.
  - A **hijacked computer-use agent is maximally harmful** — it can be steered to do anything the user could, with the user's access. The blast radius is the whole computer/web.
So computer use is the category where **capability and danger are both maximal** — which is why it gets the **strongest safety treatment** (untrusted-input doctrine, per-step safety on every action, least-privilege, allowlist navigation, human-in-the-loop for high-stakes — Lesson 27) and why **its safety can't lag its capability**. The synthesis: computer use **converges perception, grounding, and safety into the most general agent** — able to operate any software (most capable) but acting irreversibly on an adversarial surface where injection is unsolved (most dangerous) — so it demands the **full agent-engineering and security stack** (per-step safety, containment, verification, human-in-the-loop) precisely because its power is matched by its risk. It's the clearest case of the phase's central tension: **capability without reliability/safety engineering is unsafe to ship** (Lesson 31) — and for computer use, that engineering (per-step safety, defense-in-depth) is non-negotiable.
